article_title: JWST Maps Buckyballs Concentrated in a Thin Spherical Shell Around a Dying Star, 16 Years After Their First Detection in Space

content_preview.title: JWST Maps Buckyballs Concentrated in a Thin Spherical Shell Around a Dying Star, 16 Years After Their First Detection in Space
content_preview.summary: A Western University team led by Jan Cami used JWST's MIRI instrument to image planetary nebula Tc 1, finding C60 buckyballs confined to a hollow shell around the white dwarf and revealing an unexplained question-mark structure at the center.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have produced the first detailed picture of where cosmic buckyballs live. According to a [Western University announcement](https://news.westernu.ca/2026/04/jwst-buckyballs/) on April 22, 2026, a team led by physics and astronomy professor Jan Cami imaged the planetary nebula Tc 1, located more than 10,000 light-years away in the constellation Ara, and found that the C60 fullerene molecules nicknamed "buckyballs" are concentrated in a ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Solid News article (634 words). Clear structure, well-attributed throughout. Every direct quote and specific factual claim is verified verbatim against snapshot text.

source_verification.https://news.westernu.ca/2026/04/jwst-bu: source-0.html — Verified verbatim: Tc 1 in Ara constellation, 10,000+ light-years, MIRI nine filters 5.6-25.5 microns, 'upside-down question mark' feature, IFU spectroscopy, 'structures we're seeing now are breathtaking' Cami quote, Morgan Giese leading C60 analysis, thin spherical shell finding.
source_verification.https://phys.org/news/2026-04-astronomer: Source bot-blocked (403) — snapshot failed. All claims attributed to Phys.org (MIRI nine filters, 'buckyballs arranged like one giant buckyball' Giese quote, 1985 lab discovery, 2010 Spitzer detection) are independently corroborated by Western University, Scientific American, and Space.com snapshots.
source_verification.https://www.scientificamerican.com/artic: source-2.html — Verified verbatim: Tc 1 ~10,000 light-years in Ara, white dwarf at center, 'odd structure that resembles an upside down question mark' with origin still a mystery, Cami's 'breathtaking' quote, IFU spectroscopy.
source_verification.https://www.space.com/astronomy/stars/ja: source-3.html — Verified verbatim: 'first-ever detailed view of a planetary nebula', models cannot predict C60 infrared emissions ('None of our models actually correctly predicts what the correct emission would be'), Giese 'buckyballs arranged like one giant buckyball' quote, 2010 Spitzer detection.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified. Quotes are verbatim. The Giese quote is attributed to Phys.org (which 403'd) but the same wording appears in Space.com snapshot, so the attribution is functionally correct even if redundant. The 'first detailed examination at this resolution' phrasing paraphrases Space.com's 'first-ever detailed view of a planetary nebula' fairly.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, professional. Includes appropriate 'What We Don't Know' framing. No sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: Not previously covered. Cross-references prior JWST coverage (TOI-5205b article from 2026-04) with internal link, building knowledge-base continuity.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Strong science reporting with full source corroboration. The Phys.org bot-block is non-blocking because every claim attributed to it is independently verified in another snapshot. Internal cross-reference to prior JWST article is a good archive-building pattern.
